content.full_text:
NATIONAL ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT & LAW CENTER
1950 ADDISON STREET • BERKELEY, CALIFORNIA 94704
415•548•2600


September 11, 1991

California Fair Political
Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807
Attn: Jonathan Rothman

Re: A-88-422

Dear Mr. Rothman:

The City of Berkeley requested an opinion regarding the inclusion
of the South Berkeley Community Housing Development Corporation
within the definition of a local government agency in 1988
resulting in the opinion referenced above, finding the organization
to be such an agency.

At this time I would request a review of whether or not the
organization remains a local government agency in light of changes
in circumstances and what circumstances, if any, might change its
status in the future. The original decision relied on the four
part test in In re Siegel (1977) 3FPPC Ops. 62. The following are
the changes I am aware of and are organized by the structure of the
original opinion including the criteria of In re Siegel.

FACTS

The original opinion indicates that a city appointed committee
recommended the formation of a new corporation and that the
committee be the board of directors of that corporation. In the
intervening three years there has been turnover on the board of
directors. Currently only 5 of the original 11 board members are
still in office. The City has had no role in appointing the
replacement board members. This function is carried out by the
remaining directors. The board is currently contemplating one of
its original purposes, which was to bring residents of the property
onto the board as members, and to turnover control of the board to
the residents within a fixed period of time, making the property a
limited equity cooperative as training and experience permit.

Jonathan Rothman                    -2-                    September 11, 1991

ANALYSIS

Whether the impetus for formation of the corporation originated
with a government agency.

No new facts, other than the change in board membership and
appointment authority, have surfaced since the original opinion.

Whether the corporation is substantially funded by, or its primary
source of funds is, a government agency.

Initially the housing development corporation received funding in
the form of a loan for its predevelopment work from the City of
Berkeley. That funding was the sole source of support at the time
of the original opinion. Since that time it has received funding
from numerous other sources. The following is a current listing of
funding sources:

           $804,154          Loan, City of Berkeley
            500,000          Loan, City of Berkeley
            425,000          Grant, City of Berkeley
         1,729,154          Total Funding, City of Berkeley

         1,300,000          Private Financing
            950,000          Tax Credit Syndication Proceeds
         1,280,000          Loan, State of California
         3,530,000          Total Funding, Other Sources

         5,259,154          Total Development Cost

In addition the city is providing the land under a long term lease
for only $1 per year. The financing package appraised the value of
the leasehold interest at somewhere in the area of $200,000.

This would indicate that the city is still a funder of the housing
development corporation but no longer the primary one. It is
somewhat unclear to me what it means to be "substantially funded
by" the City.

Whether one of the principal purposes for the formation of the
corporation is to provide services or undertake obligations which
public agencies are legally and traditionally authorized to
perform.

The original opinion indicates that the housing development
corporation meets this test because affordable housing is involved.
The opinion cites Government Code Section 37364 giving cities the
authority to utilize real property to provide affordable housing to
families of low or moderate income. No change has occurred here.
It should be noted, however, that only a portion of the units from
the beginning were designed to be affordable. It has always been
intended to be a mixed income project with more than half of the

Jonathan Rothman                -3-                September 11, 1991

units set at market rate.  Does that make a difference?

Whether the corporation is treated as a public entity by other
statutory provisions.

The original opinion indicated that the housing development
corporation failed to meet this test but that not all tests had to
be met for an entity to qualify as a local government agency. I am
aware of no new circumstances that would bring the housing
development corporation within this further test.

Conclusions

The original opinion makes clear that not all criteria have to be
met.  Clearly the fourth is not necessary.  The opinion is silent
on how many and in what fashion the other criteria have to be met
for there to be a finding of inclusion as a local government
agency.

The impetus test historically would indicate some form of control
over the organization.  If the impetus test means only that
historically there was control over the origin of the corporation
rather than continuing control, it should not be enough alone to
bring the organization within the definition of a local government
agency.  If the organization is no longer subject to any control by
local government and starts doing things totally different than
what it was originally intended, it sounds difficult to believe that
it would still be a local government agency.

Here the facts would indicate that the City no longer controls who
serves on the board and that a majority of the City's appointments
are no longer serving.  Is this test met forever if it is met
initially, or does the real force of the test cease once the City
no longer exercises control?

The control becomes most tenuous when the local residents of the
project, none of whom were appointed by the City, become a majority
of the board.  How does the board function then?  This control by
the residents was the intent of both of the original board and the
City.  Each of the resident board members will fall under very
strict interpretation rules as to any conflict of interest because
they rent within the project.  It will be impossible to pass many
necessary resolutions.  What solutions to this problem can your
office suggest?

The "substantially funded by" test may no longer be met.  The City
is not the primary funder of the organization.  Although the City
still provides substantial funds, the test is whether it is
"substantially funded by" the City, which may connote something
closer to the notion of being the primary funder.  If this test now
or in the future is no longer met, is that sufficient to take the
organization out from under local government agency status, or are

Jonathan Rothman                -4-                September 11, 1991

the first and third criteria sufficient by themselves to make the
organization a local government agency?

The third criterion would seem to have little weight by itself. If
it is sufficient that the City have authority to carry out the
activities of the nonprofit, as the original opinion would seem to
say, this criterion would seem just a qualifier of the first two.
The City is also authorized and traditionally has conveyed property
to historical associations and low income individuals. No one
would argue, I assume, that nonprofits that convey property to
these types of individuals are inherently local government agencies
without meeting the other tests. The City is authorized to operate
a motor pool.     If it contracts with a private corporation
established by a local businessman in part to provide services to
the City, does that make the private corporation a local government
agency?   It is the conjunction of the third criterion with the
first two would seem to make it important. The first two appear to
be, and logically should be, the most important for a finding that
the nonprofit is a local government agency. If either or both of
the first two criteria have changed, the fact that housing is
involved should not be enough to perpetuate the local government
agency status.

The question then becomes whether the nonprofit still meets the
second test and the real intent of the first test, and whether the
first test, without the second test, could always bring this
nonprofit within the definition of a local government agency. The
nonprofit would appreciate an indication of whether the impetus
test will always tar it with the status of being a local government
agency, because it currently faces the prospect of not being able
to function as more and more board members, as residents of the
property, will have conflicts of interest under the FPPC test but
not under the Corporations Code.

I appreciate your consideration of this request for an opinion.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
DAVID H. KIRKPATRICK
Attorney at Law

sbhdc5.wp

sections.question: Whether the South Berkeley Community Housing Development Corporation remains a local government agency in light of changes in circumstances, including: (1) board membership turnover with City no longer appointing replacements; (2) diversified funding sources with City no longer the primary funder; (3) planned transition of board control to residents; and (4) potential conflicts of interest issues under FPPC rules as resident board members increase. Specifically, whether the 'impetus test' requires continuing control or only historical origin; whether the 'substantially funded by' test is still met; whether the third criterion has sufficient weight alone; and what solutions exist for conflicts of interest as residents become board majority.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether South Berkeley Community Housing Development Corporation, originally designated as a local government agency under the In re Sieqel test, retains that status given changes in board appointment authority, funding diversification, and planned resident control transition.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from an attorney, not an FPPC response. The document contains detailed FACTS and ANALYSIS sections but no CONCLUSION or SHORT ANSWER from FPPC, as this is the initial request. The question_synthetic field provides comprehensive coverage of the complex multi-part inquiry. OCR quality is generally good with minor formatting irregularities.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether the South Berkeley Community Housing Development Corporation remains a local government agency in light of changes in circumstances, including: (1) board membership turnover with City no longer appointing replacements; (2) diversified funding sources with City no longer the primary funder; (3) planned transition of board control to residents; and (4) potential conflicts of interest issues under FPPC rules as resident board members increase. Specifically, whether the 'impetus test' requires continuing control or only historical origin; whether the 'substantially funded by' test is still met; whether the third criterion has sufficient weight alone; and what solutions exist for conflicts of interest as residents become board majority.

embedding.summary: An attorney representing South Berkeley Community Housing Development Corporation requests FPPC review of whether the organization continues to qualify as a local government agency, given significant changes since the original 1988 opinion: board turnover with no City appointments of replacements, diversified funding sources reducing City's role to non-primary funder, and planned transition of board control to residents. The attorney analyzes each prong of the In re Sieqel test and raises questions about the test's application to changing circumstances and potential conflicts of interest issues.
